Creating the bayesdir R package 1 Introduction

Creating the bayesdir R package Sheng Jiang 2026-05-26 1 Introduction This package implements bayesdir, Bayesian directional data analysis, including model 1, model 2. The documentation and package are both created using one simple command: litr::render("index.Rmd",output_format = litr_gitbook(minimal_eval = TRUE)) ## install.packages("./bayesdir/", repos = NULL, type="source") This line is purely for testing (to be deleted later!). # Complete development workflow my_load <- function(){ litr::render("index.Rmd", output_format = litr::litr_gitbook(minimal_eval = TRUE)) devtools::document("./bayesdir") # Update documentation devtools::load_all("./bayesdir") } my_load() # Step 2: Check the package (optional but recommended) devtools::check("./bayesdir", document = FALSE) # Step 3: Install the package locally for full installation devtools::install("./bayesdir") # Step 4: Load the installed package (optional, if you want to test installation) library(bayesdir) setwd("~/repos/bayesdir/") ## 1. Delete the auto-generated _main.Rmd file unlink("_main.Rmd") unlink("_bookdown_files", recursive = TRUE) if(!dir.create("bayesdir")){ unlink("bayesdir", recursive = TRUE) } ## 2. Re-run the render command litr::render( "index.Rmd", output_format = litr::litr_gitbook(minimal_eval = TRUE) ) ## 3. Document and load the package devtools::document("bayesdir") devtools::load_all("bayesdir")

2 Package setup The DESCRIPTION file is created using this code. usethis::create_package( path = ".", fields = list( Package = params$package_name, Version = "0.0.0.9000", Title = "bayesdir", Description = "Implements Bayesian directional data analysis for von Mises-Fisher models and related methods.", `Authors@R` = person( given = "Sheng", family = "Jiang", email = "jiangsheng@cuhk.edu.cn", role = c("aut", "cre") ) ) ) usethis::use_mit_license(copyright_holder = "Sheng Jiang") The following is what will show up when someone types package?bayesdir in the console. #' bayesdir #' #' This package implements the `bayesdir` method. #' #' @docType package This package will have some dependancies: ##library(ggplot2) ##usethis::use_package("tidyverse", type = "depends") ## usethis::use_package("ggplot2") usethis::use_package("ggplot2", type = "imports") usethis::use_package("MASS", type = "imports") usethis::use_package("parallel", type = "imports") usethis::use_package("pbmcapply",type = "imports") usethis::use_package("stats", type = "imports") usethis::use_package("rstiefel", type = "imports") usethis::use_package("tidyverse", type = "imports") usethis::use_pipe(export=TRUE) #' @importFrom parallel mclapply #' @importFrom stats quantile rnorm sd uniroot #' @importFrom rstiefel rmf.vector #' @importFrom pbmcapply pbmclapply #' @importFrom magrittr %>% NULL

3 Helpers functions These are some helper functions that are needed for the posterior computation These functions are not documented for now. (TODO: do this). #' Compute the function A(kappa) = I_{p/2}(kappa) / I_{p/2-1}(kappa) #' for the von Mises–Fisher distribution. #' @param kappa concentration parameter (non‑negative scalar) #' @param p space dimension (default = 2) #' @return scalar value of A(kappa) #' @export get_Ap <- function(kappa, p = 2) { if (kappa < 1e-6) return(0) besselI(kappa, p/2, expon.scaled = TRUE) / besselI(kappa, p/2 - 1, expon.scaled = TRUE) } #' Compute the score function s(eta, x) for vMF. #' @param eta natural parameter vector (length p) #' @param x unit vector observation (length p) #' @param p space dimension (default = 2) #' @return score vector of length p #' @export score_fn <- function(eta, x, p = 2) { kappa <- sqrt(sum(eta^2)) if (kappa < 1e-6) return(x) expected <- get_Ap(kappa, p) * eta / kappa x - expected } #' Fisher information matrix for the vMF model #' #' @param C natural parameter vector (length p) #' @param p dimension (defaults to length(C)) #' @return p x p Fisher information matrix #' @export get_FIM_vmf <- function(C, p = length(C)) { kappa <- sqrt(sum(C^2)) I_p <- diag(p) if (kappa < 1e-6) return(I_p / p) mu <- C / kappa Ap <- get_Ap(kappa, p) Ap_prime <- 1 - Ap^2 - ((p - 1) / kappa) * Ap mu_mu <- mu %*% t(mu) Ap_prime * mu_mu + (Ap / kappa) * (I_p - mu_mu) } #' Compute the sum of weights sum_{t = start}^{end} 1/(t + N)^2. #' @param n number of observed data points #' @param M number of iterations #' @export #' Helper to compute weights sums compute_weights <- function(n, M) { t_early <- 1:M W_nm <- sum(1 / (n + t_early)^2) # Infinite sum R_nm = sum_{t=M+1}^\infty 1/(n+t)^2 approx 1/(n+M) R_nm <- 1 / (n + M) list(W_nm = W_nm, R_nm = R_nm) } #' Compute MLE given data X #' @param X nrow = p ncol = number of observation #' @return MLE as a p-dim vector #' @export mle_vmf <- function(X){ n <- ncol(X) sum_vec <- rowSums(X) # 2-dim vector sum Robs <- sqrt(sum(sum_vec^2)) Rbar <- Robs / n mu_hat <- atan2(sum_vec[2], sum_vec[1]) if (Rbar < 0.001) { kappa_hat <- 0 } else if (Rbar > 0.999) { kappa_hat <- 1 / (2 * (1 - Rbar)) } else { kappa_hat <- stats::uniroot(function(k) get_Ap(k, 2) - Rbar, c(0.001, 100))$root } c_hat <- kappa_hat * c(cos(mu_hat), sin(mu_hat)) return(c_hat) }
5 Predictive Resampler 5.1 The main sampler 5.2 Hybrid Method and related code

5 Predictive Resampler 5.1 The main sampler The main Gibbs sampler is called run_predresamp_vmf(). #' Run a single martingale posterior chain (no preconditioner). #' @param c0 initial natural parameter vector(use MLE) #' @param N number of observed data points #' @param M number of iteration #' @return A list with components: #' \item{c_final}{final natural parameter vector after possible correction} #' \item{mu_final}{mean direction (angle in radians if p=2)} #' \item{kappa_final}{concentration parameter} #' \item{x_chain}{all new x generated} #' \item{c_chain}{all new c generated} #'@export run_single_martingale <- function(c0, N, M = 20,save_path = FALSE) { p <- length(c0) c_curr <- c0 V_mat <- matrix(0, p, p) # accumulated un‑preconditioned score outer products total_w <- 0 if (save_path) { x_chain <- matrix(0, p, M) c_chain <- matrix(0, p, M) } # main loop w_cur <- compute_weights(N,M)$W_nm for (t in 1:M) { x_new <- rstiefel::rmf.vector(c_curr) s <- score_fn(c_curr, x_new, p) wt <- 1 / (t + N-1) total_w <- total_w + wt^2 V_mat <- V_mat + wt^2 * get_FIM_vmf(c_curr,p) c_curr <- c_curr + wt * s if (save_path) { x_chain[, t] <- x_new c_chain[, t] <- c_curr } } # Variance correction for first m step safe_sym_inverse <- function(M, tol = 1e-10) { eig <- eigen((M + t(M)) / 2, symmetric = TRUE) vals_inv <- 1 / pmax(eig$values, tol) eig$vectors %*% diag(vals_inv) %*% t(eig$vectors) } delta <- c_curr - c0 A <- total_w * safe_sym_inverse(V_mat, tol = 1e-10) c_curr <- c0 + A %*% delta # ---- Convert to polar coordinates (mu, kappa) for p=2---- kappa_final <- sqrt(sum(c_curr^2)) if (p == 2) { mu_final <- atan2(c_curr[2], c_curr[1]) if (mu_final < 0) mu_final <- mu_final + 2 * pi } else { mu_final <-(1/kappa_final) * c_curr } res <- list( c_final = c_curr, mu_final = mu_final, kappa_final = kappa_final ) if (save_path) { res$x_chain <- x_chain res$c_chain <- c_chain } res } #' run B chains for empirical density #' @param X data dim = c(p,n) #' @param B expected sample size #' @param M number of iteration #' @param n_cores number of cores used in mclapply #' @param save_chain whether to save the entire chain. #' @export run_predresamp_vmf <- function(X,B = 5000, M = 50, n_cores = 1,return_chains = FALSE){ n <- ncol(X) p <- nrow(X) c0 <- mle_vmf(X) chains <- parallel::mclapply(1:B, function(b) { run_single_martingale(c0 = c0, N = n, M = M, save_path = return_chains) }, mc.cores = n_cores) c_sam <- sapply(chains, `[[`, "c_final") kappa_sam <-sapply(chains,`[[`, "kappa_final") mu_sam <-sapply(chains,`[[`, "mu_final") sample_list <- list( c_sam = c_sam, mu_sam = mu_sam, kappa_sam = kappa_sam ) out <- list(sample = sample_list) if (return_chains) { out$chain <- chains } out } 5.2 Hybrid Method and related code #' Run the early path for a single chain (Step 1 of Hybrid Method) #' @param c0 initial natural parameter (MLE) #' @param n observed sample size #' @param M predictive steps #' @return A list containing displacement D and quadratic variation Q #' @export run_early_path <- function(c0, n, M) { p <- length(c0) c_curr <- c0 Q_mat <- matrix(0, p, p) for (t in 1:M) { gamma_nt <- 1 / (n + t) # Sample from predictive distribution x_new <- rstiefel::rmf.vector(c_curr) # Compute score s <- score_fn(c_curr, x_new, p) # Accumulate quadratic variation Q_mat <- Q_mat + (gamma_nt^2) * (s %*% t(s)) # Update parameter c_curr <- c_curr + gamma_nt * s } list( D_nm = c_curr - c0, Q_nm = Q_mat ) } #' Run Hybrid Predictive Resampling for vMF #' @param X data matrix (p x n) #' @param B number of independent chains #' @param M number of early predictive steps #' @param n_cores number of cores for parallel processing #' @export run_hybrid_vmf <- function(X, B = 5000, M = 50, n_cores = 1) { n <- ncol(X) p <- nrow(X) # 0. Initial Estimate (MLE) c0 <- mle_vmf(X) weights <- compute_weights(n, M) # 1. Run B chains to get Early Path data (Parallelized) early_results <- parallel::mclapply(1:B, function(b) { run_early_path(c0, n, M) }, mc.cores = n_cores) # Extract Ds and Qs D_list <- lapply(early_results, `[[`, "D_nm") Q_list <- lapply(early_results, `[[`, "Q_nm") # 2. Pooling (Section 1 & 5 of methodology) bar_Q_nm <- Reduce("+", Q_list) / B hat_I_nm <- bar_Q_nm / weights$W_nm # Inversion helper with stabilization safe_inv <- function(Mat) { eig <- eigen((Mat + t(Mat)) / 2) vals_inv <- 1 / pmax(eig$values, 1e-10) eig$vectors %*% diag(vals_inv) %*% t(eig$vectors) } hat_I_inv <- safe_inv(hat_I_nm) # 3. Tail Generation and Post-hoc Correction (Section 8) # Tail variance: R_nm * hat_I_nm tail_cov <- weights$R_nm * hat_I_nm # Pre-calculate matrix square root for tail sampling: Z ~ N(0, tail_cov) tail_eig <- eigen(tail_cov) tail_sqrt <- tail_eig$vectors %*% diag(sqrt(pmax(tail_eig$values, 0))) %*% t(tail_eig$vectors) c_final_samples <- sapply(1:B, function(b) { # Generate Gaussian Tail Z Z_nm <- tail_sqrt %*% rnorm(p) # Apply Correction: c_hyb = c0 + I_inv * (D + Z) c_hyb <- c0 + hat_I_inv %*% (D_list[[b]] + Z_nm) return(c_hyb) }) # 4. Transform to Polar for output kappa_sam <- apply(c_final_samples, 2, function(v) sqrt(sum(v^2))) if (p == 2) { # Convert to angles if dimension is 2 mu_sam <- apply(c_final_samples, 2, function(v) atan2(v[2], v[1])) }else{ mu_sam <- sweep(c_final_samples, 2, kappa_sam, "/")} list( samples = list( c_sam = c_final_samples, mu_sam = mu_sam, kappa_sam = kappa_sam ), info_matrix = hat_I_nm ) }

6 Simulation Study: Bayesian vMF models for current direction Data with p=2 6.1 Sythetic data for illustration with p=2 6.2 Bayesian Inference on a conjugate prior with p=2. 6.3 A simple example for MPS to help you draw graphies in the paper 6.4 Comparison between MCMC and MPS Method. 6.5 Simulation for Hybrid Method

6 Simulation Study: Bayesian vMF models for current direction Data with p=2 This vignette demonstrates a complete simulation study using bayesdir on synthetic traffic data from a grid road network. 6.1 Sythetic data for illustration with p=2 Generate data from von-mises fisher distribution. return in both cartesian coordinate and polar coordinate. Here \(\mu \in [0,2\pi]\) as an angle. \(\kappa \geq0\) as concentration. An example, For p >2, simply follow \(\texttt{rstiefel::rmf.vector}\), below we have an example. 6.2 Bayesian Inference on a conjugate prior with p=2. Used as an benchmark. See Forbes & Mardia (2015) by \(\texttt{circglmbayes}\). For the von Mises distribution on \([0,2\pi)\), \(f(\theta\mid\mu,\kappa) = \frac{1}{2\pi I_0(\kappa)}\exp\{\kappa\cos(\theta-\mu)\}\), and conjugate prior \(\pi(\mu,\kappa) \propto I_0(\kappa)^{-c}\exp\{\kappa R_0\cos(\mu-\mu_0)\},\) with hyperparameters \(c\ge0\), \(R_0\ge0\), and \(\mu_0\). The resulting joint posterior is\[\pi(\mu,\kappa\mid\theta_{1:n}) \propto I_0(\kappa)^{-(c+n)}\exp{\kappa R_n\cos(\mu-\bar\theta_n)}\] where the posterior resultant length \(R_n\) and mean direction \(\bar\theta_n\) are defined by \(R_n\cos\bar\theta_n = R_0\cos\mu_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n}\cos\theta_i\) and \(R_n\sin\bar\theta_n = R_0\sin\mu_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sin\theta_i\). We sample from the posterior using a two‑step Gibbs sampler. Given current values \((\mu,\kappa)\), Draw \(\mu\mid\kappa,\theta\) from a von Mises distribution with mean direction \(\bar\theta_n\) and concentration \(\kappa R_n\). Draw \(\kappa\mid\mu,\theta\) from the full conditional \[ \pi(\kappa\mid\mu,\theta) \propto I_0(\kappa)^{-\eta}\exp\{-\eta\beta_0\kappa\}, \] where \(\eta = c+n\) and \(\beta_0 = -\frac{R_n}{\eta}\cos(\mu-\bar\theta_n)\). Here \(\mu \in [0,2\pi]\) as an angle. \(\kappa \geq0\) as concentration. Also, \(\texttt{theta}\) as an vector with each scalar \(\theta_i\in [0, 2\pi]\) and dimension n. Below we will show an brief example. Visualize Result after fitting data, 6.3 A simple example for MPS to help you draw graphies in the paper Though \(\texttt{bayesdir::run_predresamp_vmf()}\) in \(\texttt{bayesdir()}\) Visualize Result after fitting data, For simplicity, we only perform correction on last point in \(\texttt{bayesdir::run_predresamp_vmf()}\). and \(\texttt{bayesdir::run_single_martingale()}\). So to draw similar graphes with the paper, a modified functions are needed, as \(\texttt{run_cor_mart()}\), \(\texttt{run_cor_predresamp_vmf()}\).(If you do not want to recap this part, just ignore it.) plot function plot_trace_sideways_density <- function( paths, samples, iter = NULL, ref_value = NULL, ref_density_fun = NULL, xlab = "Forward step i", ylab = expression(hat(mu)[n+i]), main = NULL, trace_col = rgb(0.25, 0.45, 0.75, 0.06), trace_lwd = 0.5, density_fill = rgb(0.25, 0.45, 0.75, 0.30), density_border = rgb(0.20, 0.35, 0.65), density_lwd = 1.2, ref_col = "red", ref_lwd = 1.5, ref_lty = 2, ref_density_col = "red", ref_density_lwd = 2, density_width_frac = 0.22, density_x_offset_frac = 1.02, xlim_multiplier = 1.25, y_pad_frac = 0.04, cex_lab = 1.2, cex_axis = 1.0, cex_main = 1.2, cex_leg = 0.9, legend_pos = "topleft", show_legend = TRUE, show_traj_legend = FALSE, mark_initial = TRUE, initial_col = "red", initial_pch = 19, initial_cex = 1.2, initial_label = "initial point", n_value = 500, n_pos = "bottomright", n_cex = 0.8, n_col = "gray40", n_pad_x = 0.02, n_pad_y = 0.03 ) { if (!is.matrix(paths)) paths <- as.matrix(paths) if (!is.numeric(paths)) stop("`paths` must be numeric.") if (!is.numeric(samples)) stop("`samples` must be numeric.") if (is.null(iter)) iter <- seq_len(ncol(paths)) - 1 if (length(iter) != ncol(paths)) { stop("`iter` must have length equal to `ncol(paths)`.") } x_start <- min(iter) x_end <- max(iter) x_width <- x_end - x_start if (x_width <= 0) stop("`iter` must span a positive range.") y_range <- range(c(as.vector(paths), samples, ref_value), finite = TRUE) y_pad <- y_pad_frac * diff(y_range) if (!is.finite(y_pad) || y_pad == 0) y_pad <- 0.1 y_range <- y_range + c(-y_pad, y_pad) dens <- density(samples, n = 512) y_grid <- seq(y_range[1], y_range[2], length.out = 500) ref_density <- NULL if (!is.null(ref_density_fun)) ref_density <- ref_density_fun(y_grid) dens_scale <- x_width * density_width_frac / max(dens$y, ref_density, na.rm = TRUE) x_offset <- x_start + x_width * density_x_offset_frac x_max <- x_start + x_width * xlim_multiplier plot( NULL, xlim = c(x_start, x_max), ylim = y_range, xlab = "", ylab = ylab, main = main, xaxt = "n", cex.lab = cex_lab, cex.axis = cex_axis, cex.main = cex_main ) ticks <- pretty(c(x_start, x_end)) ticks <- ticks[ticks >= x_start & ticks <= x_end] axis(1, at = ticks, labels = ticks, lwd = 0, lwd.ticks = 1, cex.axis = cex_axis) usr <- par("usr") segments(x_start, usr[3], x_end, usr[3], xpd = FALSE) mtext(xlab, side = 1, line = 2.8, at = (x_start + x_end) / 2, cex = cex_lab) for (i in seq_len(nrow(paths))) { lines(iter, paths[i, ], col = trace_col, lwd = trace_lwd) } if (!is.null(ref_value)) { abline(h = ref_value, col = ref_col, lwd = ref_lwd, lty = ref_lty) } polygon( x_offset + dens$y * dens_scale, dens$x, col = density_fill, border = density_border, lwd = density_lwd ) if (!is.null(ref_density)) { lines( x_offset + ref_density * dens_scale, y_grid, col = ref_density_col, lwd = ref_density_lwd ) } segments(x_offset, y_range[1], x_offset, y_range[2], col = "gray60", lty = 3, lwd = 0.8) if (mark_initial) { init_x <- iter[1] init_y <- paths[1, 1] points(init_x, init_y, col = initial_col, pch = initial_pch, cex = initial_cex) } if (!is.null(n_value)) { usr <- par("usr") # c(xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax) x_range <- usr[2] - usr[1] y_range_cur <- usr[4] - usr[3] if (n_pos == "bottomright") { x_pos <- usr[2] - x_range * n_pad_x y_pos <- usr[3] + y_range_cur * n_pad_y adj <- c(1, 0) } else if (n_pos == "bottomleft") { x_pos <- usr[1] + x_range * n_pad_x y_pos <- usr[3] + y_range_cur * n_pad_y adj <- c(0, 0) } else { warning("n_pos should be 'bottomright' or 'bottomleft'. Defaulting to bottomright.") x_pos <- usr[2] - x_range * n_pad_x y_pos <- usr[3] + y_range_cur * n_pad_y adj <- c(1, 0) } text(x = x_pos, y = y_pos, labels = paste0("n = ", n_value), adj = adj, cex = n_cex, col = n_col) } if (show_legend) { leg_text <- c("True value", "MPS density", "MCMC density") leg_col <- c(ref_col, density_border, ref_density_col) leg_lty <- c(ref_lty, 1, 1) leg_lwd <- c(ref_lwd, density_lwd, ref_density_lwd) leg_pch <- c(NA, 15, NA) leg_pt.cex <- c(NA, 1.5, NA) if (show_traj_legend) { leg_text <- c("MPS trajectories", leg_text) leg_col <- c(adjustcolor(trace_col, alpha.f = 0.6), leg_col) leg_lty <- c(1, leg_lty) leg_lwd <- c(1.5, leg_lwd) leg_pch <- c(NA, leg_pch) leg_pt.cex <- c(NA, leg_pt.cex) } if (mark_initial) { leg_text <- c(leg_text, initial_label) leg_col <- c(leg_col, initial_col) leg_lty <- c(leg_lty, NA) leg_lwd <- c(leg_lwd, NA) leg_pch <- c(leg_pch, initial_pch) leg_pt.cex <- c(leg_pt.cex, 1.2) } legend( legend_pos, legend = leg_text, col = leg_col, lty = leg_lty, lwd = leg_lwd, pch = leg_pch, pt.cex = leg_pt.cex, bty = "n", cex = cex_leg ) } invisible(list( density = dens, y_range = y_range, x_offset = x_offset, dens_scale = dens_scale )) } Example for drawing graphes. set.seed(2026) #for n=100,500 #Settings nn_cores <- max(detectCores()-1,1) mu_true <- 2 kappa_true <- 4 n <- 500 # use n=500 for another graph data <- generate_vmf_data(n, mu_true = mu_true, kappa_true = kappa_true) #MCMC res_MCMC <- fit_mcmc_vmf(data$theta,n_iter=7000,burn = 5000) kappa_MCMC <- res_MCMC[,2] kappa_den <- density(kappa_MCMC) ref_fun <- approxfun(kappa_den$x, kappa_den$y, yleft = 0, yright = 0) #MPS cor_res <- run_cor_predresamp_vmf(data$X, B = 2000, M = 1000, n_cores = nn_cores, return_chains = TRUE) kappa_paths <- t(sapply(cor_res$chain, function(chain) { sqrt(colSums(chain$c_chain^2)) })) c0 <- bayesdir::mle_vmf(data$X) kappa0 <- sqrt(sum(c0^2)) kappa_paths_with_init <- cbind(kappa0, kappa_paths) kappa_last <- kappa_paths_with_init[, ncol(kappa_paths_with_init)] #Draw graph, Finally! plot_trace_sideways_density( paths = kappa_paths_with_init, samples = kappa_last, iter = 1:1001, ref_value = kappa_true, ref_density_fun = ref_fun, ref_density_col = "blue", ref_density_lwd = 2, xlab = "Forward step", ylab = "kappa", cex_leg = 0.7, n_value = n, n_cex = 0.9 ) 6.4 Comparison between MCMC and MPS Method. 7 OSCAR real-data application 7.1 Data and tuning 7.2 Helper functions 7.3 Load data and compute MLEs 7.4 Predictive resampling and post-correction 7.5 Post-corrected trace plot 7.6 Rose diagram and angular density

7 OSCAR real-data application This vignette reproduces the real-data results used in the OSCAR southern offshore compact application: the numerical summary table, the post-corrected trace-and-terminal-density figure, and the two-panel rose/density figure. The analysis uses the vMF predictive-resampling functions in our package; the small helper routines below only store full trajectories and produce the paper figures. 7.1 Data and tuning The two CSV files contains the preprocessed compact subarea observations for summer and winter, with columns y1 and y2 giving unit current-direction vectors after rotation to the common reference direction. For local testing, the code also searches the experiment folder used to prepare the paper figures. 7.2 Helper functions 7.3 Load data and compute MLEs 7.4 Predictive resampling and post-correction The chunk below reruns the full predictive-resampling experiment when rerun_mps = TRUE or when no cached result file is available. The full-path helper uses the same score, Fisher information, and affine variance correction as the exported bayesdir sampler, but stores the intermediate states needed for the trace figure. 7.5 Post-corrected trace plot 7.6 Rose diagram and angular density
